finding: Claim 5 ("PluginLoader extended with kind:desktop") introduces a cross-process architectural contradiction. PluginLoader lives in packages/opencode/src/plugin/loader.ts — the server-side Node.js process (the sidecar). It uses Node.js `import()` for module resolution, operates in the Effect/Layer/IoC container context, and resolves entries from npm packages to filesystem targets. Desktop plugins are intended to run in the Electron renderer process with SolidJS component rendering in a DOM context. These are separate processes with separate module systems and runtimes. Adding `kind: "desktop"` to PluginKind in packages/opencode/src/plugin/shared.ts (source: line 37, `export type PluginKind = "server" | "tui"`) and passing it through PluginLoader.resolve() would cause the server process to attempt resolution of desktop entrypoints in the Node.js runtime — but desktop plugin modules contain SolidJS JSX components that will fail to import in Node.js. Furthermore, readV1Plugin() in shared.ts (source: line 272-306) validates module shape for server and tui but has no desktop awareness; a desktop-only module passed through PluginLoader would silently produce no plugin. The config handoff is also unspecified: the plan says plugins load from `opencode.json desktop.plugin` but opencode.json is parsed by the server sidecar, not the Electron main or renderer process. Desktop plugin config must cross the sidecar→main→renderer IPC boundary, which requires at minimum a new IPC channel and an Electron preload method that the plan defers.
repair_path: Do not extend PluginKind with "desktop". Instead, create a separate DesktopPluginLoader in packages/desktop/src/renderer/plugin-loader.ts that lives in the renderer process and handles resolution specifically for desktop plugins. The main process can read `desktop.plugin` from opencode.json (or from electron-store) and forward it to the renderer via a new IPC channel (e.g., `get-desktop-plugin-config`). The type-level DesktopPluginModule union in @opencode-ai/plugin is correct, but the server-side loader should never be asked to resolve it. Remove claim 5 from the boundary or redefine it as "a DesktopPluginLoader in the renderer process handles desktop plugin resolution and lifecycle".

finding: DesktopPluginApi.store exposes raw electron-store get/set to all desktop plugins. The proposed DesktopPluginApi type includes `store: { get: (key: string) => unknown; set: (key: string, value: unknown) => void }` — this gives every arbitrary desktop plugin full read/write access to ALL electron-store keys, including sidecar server config (port, password), window state, theme settings, and UI preferences. There is no key scoping, no namespace isolation, no read-only vs write distinction. An unprivileged third-party plugin from npm could arbitrarily read the sidecar authentication password or corrupt window layout settings. The plan identifies "Config isolation: desktop.plugin config does not leak into the server plugin registry" as an attack surface but does not address plugin-to-plugin and plugin-to-host isolation within the store.
repair_path: Replace raw electron-store access with a scoped KV namespace per plugin. The DesktopPluginApi.store should be `{ namespace(id: string): { get: (key: string) => unknown; set: (key: string, value: unknown) => void } }` which internally prefixes keys with `plugin:${pluginId}:` so plugin A cannot read or write plugin B's data, nor access host-level store keys. Alternatively, use a separate electron-store instance per plugin. Additionally, add a `host`-level restricted store on the DesktopPluginApi that only exposes documented, safe host config values (such as theme preference) and is read-only.

finding: The plan identifies "A crashing plugin must not crash the host renderer (error boundary)" as attack surface #1 but contains NO implementation of error boundaries anywhere in the scope. The DesktopSlotRenderer component design (section 3 of the plan) is described only as "component that renders a named slot's registered component" with no mention of error boundary wrapping. In SolidJS, a runtime error in a child component during rendering will percolate up and crash the entire component tree unless explicitly caught with an ErrorBoundary. Since slot plugins are arbitrary third-party SolidJS components, any runtime error in a plugin component (null reference, missing prop, incorrect import) will crash the entire renderer process — the Electron app window becomes a white screen. This is the single most dangerous production failure mode for a slot system and it is explicitly identified but not addressed.
repair_path: Include a SolidJS ErrorBoundary implementation in the plan scope. Add a SlotErrorBoundary component in plugin-context.tsx that wraps each <DesktopSlotRenderer> output. The boundary should: (1) catch errors during rendering and lifecycle, (2) render a small fallback UI (a styled box with the slot name and an error indicator), (3) optionally surface the error to the DevTools console without crashing the host, and (4) preserve the host frame (sidebar, titlebar) intact. The plan should also add a mechanism for the host to decide whether to show the fallback or completely remove the slot. This is not deferrable — it is core infrastructure for the slot system, not a hardening layer added later.

finding: The evidence plan is implementation-complete checkmarks (typecheck, build, plugin loads, slot renders) with no falsification tests or failure-mode evidence. The plan itself identifies four attack surfaces (crash isolation, mutual exclusion, config isolation, preload pollution) but the evidence plan has zero tests for any of them. Falsifications that should be in the evidence plan: (1) load a plugin that exports both desktop and server — verify the union type rejects it at the type level AND the runtime enforces it; (2) have a slot component throw during render — verify the host app stays alive; (3) load a plugin that tries to access `localStorage`/`fetch` — verify it has no elevated Electron privileges; (4) set a non-existent opencode.json `desktop.plugin` entry — verify graceful no-op instead of crash; (5) readV1Plugin call with a desktop-only module — verify it returns undefined cleanly rather than throwing.
repair_path: Add a falsification section to the evidence plan with explicit tests for each attack surface. At minimum: a type-level test (TypeScript `expectError` or `@ts-expect-error` pattern), a unit test for the runtime mutual exclusion guard, a renderer integration test for error boundary behavior (can use vitest + jsdom or @solidjs/testing-library), and a store scoping test that proves one plugin cannot read another plugin's store namespace. Each falsification must be concrete, automatable, and runnable in CI.

finding: Claims 1-3 (DesktopPlugin type, DesktopHostSlotMap, DesktopSlotPlugin registration) are achievable as typed artifacts in packages/plugin/src/desktop.ts — they use only solid-js types with no @opentui dependency. However, claim 4 (DesktopPluginModule added to PluginModule union) has a runtime enforcement gap. The current PluginModule type in packages/plugin/src/index.ts (line 76-78) is `{ server: Plugin; tui?: never }`. The plan proposes adding a third union member with `desktop: DesktopPlugin` and `server?: never, tui?: never`. This compiles at the type level, but the runtime validator readV1Plugin() (packages/opencode/src/plugin/shared.ts, lines 272-306) only checks `"server" in value` and `"tui" in value` and throws `"must default export either server() or tui(), not both"`. A desktop-only module `{ desktop: () => {} }` would hit the `kind === "server"` branch with `server === undefined` and throw "must default export an object with server()". If readV1Plugin is called with kind "tui", it would throw "must default export an object with tui()". The runtime does not know about "desktop" and would reject valid desktop plugin modules as invalid. This makes claim 4 unfalsifiable as written — the type says desktop xor server xor tui, but the validation code rejects desktop modules entirely.
repair_path: Either: (A) do NOT add "desktop" to PluginKind in shared.ts and do NOT call readV1Plugin for desktop plugins — desktop plugins are loaded by the renderer-side DesktopPluginLoader, not the server-side PluginLoader, so they never enter readV1Plugin. This makes claim 4 true in practice (desktop plugins are never validated by the server validator). Or (B) if the types must share the same validation pipeline, update readV1Plugin to accept "desktop" as a PluginKind, add a `desktop` property check analogous to server/tui, and skip the mutual exclusivity check between desktop and server/tui only when kind is explicitly "desktop". Option (A) is architecturally cleaner and avoids the cross-process contradiction of claim 5.
